1:
"But it came to pass within a while after, in the time of wheat harvest, that Samson visited his wife with a kid; and he said, I will go in to my wife into the chamber. But her father would not suffer him to go in." The witnesses note Samson returning to his wife with a gift, but her father refusing him entry. The witnesses note that Gill notes it was a year after, in wheat harvest, which began at Pentecost. The witnesses note that her father would not suffer him to go in. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the visited his wife — with a kid; the I will go in — seeking reconciliation; the her father refused — the door shut; the ill returns from the world — i am for peace, but they for war (Ps. 120:7).

The witnesses agree Samson, his anger cooled, came back seeking reconciliation — "Samson visited his wife with a kid... but her father would not suffer him to go in": he came with a gift, willing to be reconciled, but found the door shut against him; the refusal of the father, who had given the wife to another, set the new occasion of strife; it teaches that even readiness to be reconciled may be met with rejection by the unfaithful, and that the people of God must look for ill returns from the world, "I am for peace: but when I speak, they are for war" (Ps. 120:7), Samson refused entry to his wife.

So the witnesses make the verse would not suffer him: The witnesses note the visited his wife: with a kid. The witnesses note the I will go in: seeking reconciliation. The witnesses note the her father refused: the door shut. The witnesses note the ill returns from the world: I am for peace, but they for war (Ps. 120:7). The expositors observe the visited his wife: with a kid. The expositors observe the I will go in: seeking reconciliation. The expositors observe the her father refused: the door shut. The expositors observe the ill returns from the world: I am for peace, but they for war (Ps. 120:7). The believer is taught to mark the visited his wife, the I will go in, the her father refused, the ill returns from the world, and to leave vengeance to the LORD. The verse thus records that Samson came seeking reconciliation but was refused entry — readiness for peace met with rejection by the unfaithful, the people of God looking for ill returns from the world, I am for peace, but they are for war (Ps. 120:7).
2:
"And her father said, I verily thought that thou hadst utterly hated her; therefore I gave her to thy companion: is not her younger sister fairer than she? take her, I pray thee, instead of her." The witnesses note the father excusing his giving away the wife, and offering the younger sister. The witnesses note that Gill notes he affirmed confidently he thought Samson utterly hated her. The witnesses note that he offered the younger sister instead. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the I thought thou hadst hated her — the excuse; the I gave her to thy companion — the wrong; the her younger sister — offered instead; the the false make light — they speak vanity with flattering lips (Ps. 12:2).

The witnesses agree the father offered a poor excuse and a worse remedy — "I... thought thou hadst utterly hated her... is not her younger sister fairer? take her": he sought to cover his faithlessness with a pretext and to appease Samson with another daughter; the shifting excuse and the careless offer show the lightness with which the ungodly regard their wrongs and their bonds; it teaches that the world makes light of faithfulness and would buy off the wronged with a substitute, and that the people of God find no true dealing among the false, "they speak vanity every one with his neighbour: with flattering lips... do they speak" (Ps. 12:2), the father offering the younger sister.

So the witnesses make the verse the younger sister: The witnesses note the I thought thou hadst hated her: the excuse. The witnesses note the I gave her to thy companion: the wrong. The witnesses note the her younger sister: offered instead. The witnesses note the the false make light: they speak vanity with flattering lips (Ps. 12:2). The expositors observe the I thought thou hadst hated her: the excuse. The expositors observe the I gave her to thy companion: the wrong. The expositors observe the her younger sister: offered instead. The expositors observe the the false make light: they speak vanity with flattering lips (Ps. 12:2). The believer is taught to mark the I thought thou hadst hated her, the I gave her to thy companion, the her younger sister, the the false make light, and to leave vengeance to the LORD. The verse thus records that the father excused his faithlessness and offered the younger sister — the lightness with which the ungodly regard their bonds, the world buying off the wronged with a substitute, speaking vanity with flattering lips (Ps. 12:2).
3:
"And Samson said concerning them, Now shall I be more blameless than the Philistines, though I do them a displeasure." The witnesses note Samson reckoning himself blameless in the vengeance he would now take. The witnesses note that Gill notes he said this respecting them, either within himself or openly. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set his words, now shall I be blameless. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the now shall I be blameless — his reckoning; the than the Philistines — their treachery; the though I do them a displeasure — the vengeance; the vengeance is God's — avenge not yourselves (Rom. 12:19).

The witnesses agree Samson held himself justified by the wrong done him — "Now shall I be more blameless than the Philistines, though I do them a displeasure": the repeated treachery of the Philistines gave him, he reckoned, a clear ground to requite them; though private vengeance is not for God's people, Samson stood here as the appointed judge and instrument against the oppressors of Israel; it teaches that the LORD raised him to chastise the Philistines, yet warns that men are not to take vengeance for themselves, "avenge not yourselves... for it is written, Vengeance is mine; I will repay, saith the Lord" (Rom. 12:19), Samson reckoning himself blameless against the Philistines.

So the witnesses make the verse more blameless: The witnesses note the now shall I be blameless: his reckoning. The witnesses note the than the Philistines: their treachery. The witnesses note the though I do them a displeasure: the vengeance. The witnesses note the vengeance is God's: avenge not yourselves (Rom. 12:19). The expositors observe the now shall I be blameless: his reckoning. The expositors observe the than the Philistines: their treachery. The expositors observe the though I do them a displeasure: the vengeance. The expositors observe the vengeance is God's: avenge not yourselves (Rom. 12:19). The believer is taught to mark the now shall I be blameless, the than the Philistines, the though I do them a displeasure, the vengeance is God's, and to leave vengeance to the LORD. The verse thus records that Samson reckoned himself blameless in requiting the Philistines — the appointed judge against the oppressors, yet the warning that men are not to take private vengeance, avenge not yourselves, vengeance is the Lord's (Rom. 12:19).
4:
"And Samson went and caught three hundred foxes, and took firebrands, and turned tail to tail, and put a firebrand in the midst between two tails." The witnesses note Samson catching three hundred foxes and tying firebrands between their tails. The witnesses note that Gill notes this should not be thought incredible, since Canaan abounded with foxes. The witnesses note that he turned tail to tail with a firebrand between. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the caught three hundred foxes — the means; the took firebrands — for the fire; the tail to tail — the device; the all at God's service — whatsoever the LORD pleased, that did he (Ps. 135:6).

The witnesses agree Samson devised an ingenious means of judgment on the Philistines' substance — "caught three hundred foxes... and put a firebrand in the midst between two tails": by the frightened beasts he would carry fire through all the standing harvest; the strange and clever device shows the resourcefulness God gave his servant to smite the enemy; it teaches that the LORD furnishes his instruments with means to accomplish his purpose, even by unlikely creatures, all things being at his service, "whatsoever the LORD pleased, that did he" (Ps. 135:6), Samson sending the foxes with firebrands.

So the witnesses make the verse foxes and firebrands: The witnesses note the caught three hundred foxes: the means. The witnesses note the took firebrands: for the fire. The witnesses note the tail to tail: the device. The witnesses note the all at God's service: whatsoever the LORD pleased, that did he (Ps. 135:6). The expositors observe the caught three hundred foxes: the means. The expositors observe the took firebrands: for the fire. The expositors observe the tail to tail: the device. The expositors observe the all at God's service: whatsoever the LORD pleased, that did he (Ps. 135:6). The believer is taught to mark the caught three hundred foxes, the took firebrands, the tail to tail, the all at God's service, and to leave vengeance to the LORD. The verse thus records that Samson sent three hundred foxes with firebrands through the harvest — the resourcefulness God gave his servant to smite the enemy, all creatures at his service, whatsoever the LORD pleased that did he (Ps. 135:6).
5:
"And when he had set the brands on fire, he let them go into the standing corn of the Philistines, and burnt up both the shocks, and also the standing corn, with the vineyards and olives." The witnesses note the foxes spreading fire through the Philistines' corn, vineyards, and olives. The witnesses note that Gill notes the foxes, frightened by the fire near them, ran into the standing corn. The witnesses note that the shocks, standing corn, vineyards, and olives were burnt. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the set the brands on fire — the foxes loosed; the the standing corn — of the Philistines; the the shocks and vineyards — and olives; the the wealth to ashes — the increase departs in the day of wrath (Job 20:28).

The witnesses agree the fire wrought a sweeping destruction of the Philistines' wealth — "burnt up both the shocks, and... the standing corn, with the vineyards and olives": the whole harvest, the staff of their life and the source of their riches, went up in flame; the consuming of the enemy's substance shows how God can strip the oppressor of his goods in a moment; it teaches that the LORD turns the wealth of the wicked to ashes, the harvest of the ungodly burned, "the increase of his house shall depart... in the day of his wrath" (Job 20:28), the Philistines' corn and vineyards burnt.

So the witnesses make the verse burnt up the corn: The witnesses note the set the brands on fire: the foxes loosed. The witnesses note the the standing corn: of the Philistines. The witnesses note the the shocks and vineyards: and olives. The witnesses note the the wealth to ashes: the increase departs in the day of wrath (Job 20:28). The expositors observe the set the brands on fire: the foxes loosed. The expositors observe the the standing corn: of the Philistines. The expositors observe the the shocks and vineyards: and olives. The expositors observe the the wealth to ashes: the increase departs in the day of wrath (Job 20:28). The believer is taught to mark the set the brands on fire, the the standing corn, the the shocks and vineyards, the the wealth to ashes, and to leave vengeance to the LORD. The verse thus records that the fire burnt up the Philistines' corn, vineyards, and olives — God stripping the oppressor of his goods in a moment, the wealth of the wicked turned to ashes, the increase departing in the day of his wrath (Job 20:28).
6:
"Then the Philistines said, Who hath done this? And they answered, Samson... because he had taken his wife, and given her to his companion. And the Philistines came up, and burnt her and her father with fire." The witnesses note the Philistines, learning the cause, burning Samson's wife and her father. The witnesses note that Gill notes they inquired who was the author of the mischief. The witnesses note that Clarke notes they burnt her and her father, the very thing first threatened. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the who hath done this — they asked; the Samson — for his wife given away; the burnt her and her father — the dreaded fire; the the feared evil comes — the fear of the wicked shall come upon him (Prov. 10:24).

The witnesses agree a dreadful irony fell on the wife — "the Philistines... burnt her and her father with fire": the very doom her own people had threatened to make her betray Samson (Judg. 14:15) now overtook her at their hands; the fire she had feared and sought to escape by treachery consumed her in the end; it teaches that the sin men commit to escape evil often brings that very evil upon them, and that there is no safety in betraying for fear, "the fear of the wicked, it shall come upon him" (Prov. 10:24), the wife burned by the Philistines.

So the witnesses make the verse burnt her with fire: The witnesses note the who hath done this: they asked. The witnesses note the Samson: for his wife given away. The witnesses note the burnt her and her father: the dreaded fire. The witnesses note the the feared evil comes: the fear of the wicked shall come upon him (Prov. 10:24). The expositors observe the who hath done this: they asked. The expositors observe the Samson: for his wife given away. The expositors observe the burnt her and her father: the dreaded fire. The expositors observe the the feared evil comes: the fear of the wicked shall come upon him (Prov. 10:24). The believer is taught to mark the who hath done this, the Samson, the burnt her and her father, the the feared evil comes, and to leave vengeance to the LORD. The verse thus records that the Philistines burned Samson's wife and her father — the very fire she had feared and betrayed him to escape now consuming her, the sin done to escape evil bringing it on, the fear of the wicked coming upon him (Prov. 10:24).
7:
"And Samson said unto them, Though ye have done this, yet will I be avenged of you, and after that I will cease." The witnesses note Samson vowing one more vengeance before he would cease. The witnesses note that Gill notes they had burnt his wife and her father, thinking to appease him. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set his word, yet will I be avenged. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the though ye have done this — the cruelty; the yet will I be avenged — his resolve; the after that I will cease — the escalation; the the endless cycle — recompense no man evil for evil (Rom. 12:17-19).

The witnesses agree the cruelty only stirred Samson to further requital — "Though ye have done this, yet will I be avenged of you, and after that I will cease": the burning of the woman, far from appeasing him, kindled a fresh resolve; the escalation of the strife shows the bitter and endless cycle of vengeance among men; it teaches the misery of the unending quarrel that one wrong breeds another, and that only the leaving of vengeance to God can break the cycle, "recompense to no man evil for evil... avenge not yourselves" (Rom. 12:17-19), Samson resolving to be avenged once more.

So the witnesses make the verse I will be avenged: The witnesses note the though ye have done this: the cruelty. The witnesses note the yet will I be avenged: his resolve. The witnesses note the after that I will cease: the escalation. The witnesses note the the endless cycle: recompense no man evil for evil (Rom. 12:17-19). The expositors observe the though ye have done this: the cruelty. The expositors observe the yet will I be avenged: his resolve. The expositors observe the after that I will cease: the escalation. The expositors observe the the endless cycle: recompense no man evil for evil (Rom. 12:17-19). The believer is taught to mark the though ye have done this, the yet will I be avenged, the after that I will cease, the the endless cycle, and to leave vengeance to the LORD. The verse thus records that Samson vowed one more vengeance — the cruelty kindling fresh requital, the bitter cycle of vengeance among men, only the leaving of vengeance to God breaking it, recompense no man evil for evil (Rom. 12:17-19).
8:
"And he smote them hip and thigh with a great slaughter: and he went down and dwelt in the top of the rock Etam." The witnesses note Samson smiting the Philistines with great slaughter, then dwelling in the rock of Etam. The witnesses note that Gill notes he smote them on hip and thigh with his hands, having no weapon of war, with a great slaughter. The witnesses note that he dwelt in the top of the rock Etam. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the smote them hip and thigh — a great slaughter; the with no weapon — the strength God gave; the dwelt in the rock Etam — the refuge; the the rock a shelter — he shall set me up upon a rock (Ps. 27:5).

The witnesses agree Samson dealt the Philistines a heavy blow and withdrew to a stronghold — "he smote them hip and thigh with a great slaughter: and... dwelt in the top of the rock Etam": with no weapon but the strength God gave, he wrought a great destruction, then retired to the safety of the rock; the smiting of the oppressor and the retreat to the rock show both the power God gave and the prudence of seeking shelter; it teaches that the LORD makes his servant mighty against the foe, and that the rock is a fit figure of the refuge to which the believer retires, "he shall set me up upon a rock" (Ps. 27:5), Samson smiting the Philistines and dwelling in the rock.

So the witnesses make the verse hip and thigh: The witnesses note the smote them hip and thigh: a great slaughter. The witnesses note the with no weapon: the strength God gave. The witnesses note the dwelt in the rock Etam: the refuge. The witnesses note the the rock a shelter: he shall set me up upon a rock (Ps. 27:5). The expositors observe the smote them hip and thigh: a great slaughter. The expositors observe the with no weapon: the strength God gave. The expositors observe the dwelt in the rock Etam: the refuge. The expositors observe the the rock a shelter: he shall set me up upon a rock (Ps. 27:5). The believer is taught to mark the smote them hip and thigh, the with no weapon, the dwelt in the rock Etam, the the rock a shelter, and to leave vengeance to the LORD. The verse thus records that Samson smote the Philistines with great slaughter and dwelt in the rock Etam — God making his servant mighty against the foe, the rock a figure of the refuge to which the believer retires, set up upon a rock (Ps. 27:5).
9:
"Then the Philistines went up, and pitched in Judah, and spread themselves in Lehi." The witnesses note the Philistines invading Judah and spreading themselves in Lehi to take Samson. The witnesses note that Gill notes they went up from Palestine, which lay low on the seashore. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set them pitching in Judah and spreading in Lehi. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the Philistines went up — invaded; the pitched in Judah — the host; the spread in Lehi — to take Samson; the the righteous watched — the wicked seeketh to slay him (Ps. 37:32).

The witnesses agree the Philistines now turned their force against Judah to seize Samson — "the Philistines went up, and pitched in Judah, and spread themselves in Lehi": they invaded the land of God's people to lay hold of the one man who troubled them; the marching of a host against a single man shows both the fear Samson struck into them and the low estate of Judah, suffering the enemy to encamp in their midst; it teaches that the people of God, when sunk in subjection, must bear the invasion of the foe, and that one faithful man may be the object of all the enemy's rage, "the wicked watcheth the righteous, and seeketh to slay him" (Ps. 37:32), the Philistines spreading in Lehi.

So the witnesses make the verse pitched in Judah: The witnesses note the the Philistines went up: invaded. The witnesses note the pitched in Judah: the host. The witnesses note the spread in Lehi: to take Samson. The witnesses note the the righteous watched: the wicked seeketh to slay him (Ps. 37:32). The expositors observe the the Philistines went up: invaded. The expositors observe the pitched in Judah: the host. The expositors observe the spread in Lehi: to take Samson. The expositors observe the the righteous watched: the wicked seeketh to slay him (Ps. 37:32). The believer is taught to mark the the Philistines went up, the pitched in Judah, the spread in Lehi, the the righteous watched, and to leave vengeance to the LORD. The verse thus records that the Philistines invaded Judah and spread in Lehi to seize Samson — the host marching against one man, Judah sunk in subjection bearing the invasion, the wicked watching the righteous and seeking to slay him (Ps. 37:32).
10:
"And the men of Judah said, Why are ye come up against us? And they answered, To bind Samson are we come up, to do to him as he hath done to us." The witnesses note Judah asking the cause, and the Philistines declaring they came to bind Samson. The witnesses note that Gill notes Judah sent a deputation to know the reason of this formidable appearance. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the answer, to bind Samson and do to him as he had done. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the why are ye come up — judah asks; the to bind Samson — their purpose; the to do to him as he did — requital; the the deliverer bound — they bound Jesus (John 18:12).

The witnesses agree the Philistines' purpose was to seize the deliverer and requite him — "To bind Samson are we come up, to do to him as he hath done to us": they would lay hold on the one God had raised to free Israel, to bind and punish him; the seeking to bind the deliverer shows the enmity of the world against him whom God sends to save; it teaches that the saviour God raises up is hated and hunted by the oppressor, and points faintly to the binding of a greater Deliverer, who would be taken and bound by his enemies, "they laid hold on Jesus, and bound him" (John 18:12), the Philistines coming to bind Samson.

So the witnesses make the verse to bind Samson: The witnesses note the why are ye come up: Judah asks. The witnesses note the to bind Samson: their purpose. The witnesses note the to do to him as he did: requital. The witnesses note the the deliverer bound: they bound Jesus (John 18:12). The expositors observe the why are ye come up: Judah asks. The expositors observe the to bind Samson: their purpose. The expositors observe the to do to him as he did: requital. The expositors observe the the deliverer bound: they bound Jesus (John 18:12). The believer is taught to mark the why are ye come up, the to bind Samson, the to do to him as he did, the the deliverer bound, and to leave vengeance to the LORD. The verse thus records that the Philistines came to bind Samson and requite him — the enmity of the world against the deliverer God sends, pointing faintly to a greater Deliverer taken and bound by his enemies, they laid hold on Jesus and bound him (John 18:12).
11:
"Then three thousand men of Judah went... to the rock Etam, and said to Samson, Knowest thou not that the Philistines are rulers over us? what is this that thou hast done unto us?..." The witnesses note three thousand of Judah coming to reproach Samson for stirring up the Philistines. The witnesses note that Gill notes they went down to the cave of the rock of Etam. The witnesses note that they said, knowest thou not the Philistines rule over us? The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the three thousand of Judah — came down; the the Philistines rule over us — their bondage; the what hast thou done — the reproach; the content in chains — we were never in bondage (John 8:33).

The witnesses agree the men of Judah, sunk in subjection, blamed their own deliverer — "Knowest thou not that the Philistines are rulers over us? what is this that thou hast done?": so cowed were they that they reproached the man God raised to free them, counting his blows against the oppressor a trouble to themselves; the rebuking of the deliverer by the people he came to save shows the wretchedness of a spirit broken by long bondage, content with its chains; it teaches that a people enslaved to sin may resent the very one who would set them free, preferring ease in bondage to the cost of deliverance, "they... were not subject to the law of God" yet feared to be freed (cf. John 8:33, We were never in bondage), Judah reproaching Samson.

So the witnesses make the verse rulers over us: The witnesses note the three thousand of Judah: came down. The witnesses note the the Philistines rule over us: their bondage. The witnesses note the what hast thou done: the reproach. The witnesses note the content in chains: We were never in bondage (John 8:33). The expositors observe the three thousand of Judah: came down. The expositors observe the the Philistines rule over us: their bondage. The expositors observe the what hast thou done: the reproach. The expositors observe the content in chains: We were never in bondage (John 8:33). The believer is taught to mark the three thousand of Judah, the the Philistines rule over us, the what hast thou done, the content in chains, and to cry unto the LORD who gives water to the fainting. The verse thus records that the men of Judah reproached their own deliverer, content under the Philistine yoke — the wretchedness of a spirit broken by long bondage, resenting the one who would free them, content in their chains (John 8:33).
12:
"And they said unto him, We are come down to bind thee, that we may deliver thee into the hand of the Philistines. And Samson said unto them, Swear unto me, that ye will not fall upon me yourselves." The witnesses note Judah coming to bind Samson and deliver him to the Philistines. The witnesses note that Gill notes they were come down into the cave where he was. The witnesses note that Clarke sets the demand that they swear not to fall on him. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the come to bind thee — their purpose; the deliver thee to the Philistines — to keep their peace; the swear unto me — his demand; the the deliverer delivered up — they delivered him to Pilate (Mark 15:1).

The witnesses agree Judah was ready to bind and hand over the man God had sent — "We are come down to bind thee, that we may deliver thee into the hand of the Philistines": the people would give up their deliverer to the enemy to keep their own peace; the betrayal of the saviour by his own, to appease the oppressor, foreshadows the giving-up of a greater Deliverer by his own nation to the Gentiles; it teaches the baseness of betraying the friend of God's people to their enemies, and that the world's peace is often bought by surrendering the righteous, "the chief priests... delivered him to Pilate" (Mark 15:1), Judah binding Samson to deliver him up.

So the witnesses make the verse come to bind thee: The witnesses note the come to bind thee: their purpose. The witnesses note the deliver thee to the Philistines: to keep their peace. The witnesses note the swear unto me: his demand. The witnesses note the the deliverer delivered up: they delivered him to Pilate (Mark 15:1). The expositors observe the come to bind thee: their purpose. The expositors observe the deliver thee to the Philistines: to keep their peace. The expositors observe the swear unto me: his demand. The expositors observe the the deliverer delivered up: they delivered him to Pilate (Mark 15:1). The believer is taught to mark the come to bind thee, the deliver thee to the Philistines, the swear unto me, the the deliverer delivered up, and to cry unto the LORD who gives water to the fainting. The verse thus records that Judah came to bind Samson and deliver him to the Philistines — the betrayal of the saviour by his own to appease the oppressor, the world's peace bought by surrendering the righteous, delivered up to the enemy (Mark 15:1).
13:
"And they spake unto him, saying, No; but we will bind thee fast, and deliver thee into their hand: but surely we will not kill thee. And they bound him with two new cords..." The witnesses note Judah binding Samson with two new cords, but vowing not to kill him themselves. The witnesses note that Gill notes they would not slay him themselves; nor would the Jews put Christ to death themselves, though his betrayers. The witnesses note that they bound him with two new cords. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the we will bind thee fast — the cords; the not kill thee — their hands clean in show; the two new cords — the binding; the the betrayers' guilt — it is not lawful for us to put any to death (John 18:31).

The witnesses agree Judah bound the deliverer with new cords, yet would not themselves slay him — "we will bind thee fast, and deliver thee... but... we will not kill thee... they bound him with two new cords": Gill marks the parallel, that the Jews would not put Christ to death by their own hand, though they were "his betrayers and murderers," delivering him to others; the binding of Samson by his own people is a striking shadow of the binding and delivering-up of Christ; it teaches that the rejection of God's deliverer is the guilt of those who hand him over no less than of those who slay, "it is not lawful for us to put any man to death" (John 18:31), Samson bound with two new cords.

So the witnesses make the verse two new cords: The witnesses note the we will bind thee fast: the cords. The witnesses note the not kill thee: their hands clean in show. The witnesses note the two new cords: the binding. The witnesses note the the betrayers' guilt: it is not lawful for us to put any to death (John 18:31). The expositors observe the we will bind thee fast: the cords. The expositors observe the not kill thee: their hands clean in show. The expositors observe the two new cords: the binding. The expositors observe the the betrayers' guilt: it is not lawful for us to put any to death (John 18:31). The believer is taught to mark the we will bind thee fast, the not kill thee, the two new cords, the the betrayers' guilt, and to cry unto the LORD who gives water to the fainting. The verse thus records that Judah bound Samson with two new cords yet would not slay him — a shadow of the binding and delivering-up of Christ by his own, the guilt of those who hand over the deliverer no less than of those who slay, it is not lawful for us to put any man to death (John 18:31).
14:
"And when he came unto Lehi, the Philistines shouted against him: and the Spirit of the LORD came mightily upon him, and the cords... became as flax that was burnt with fire, and his bands loosed from off his hands." The witnesses note the Spirit coming mightily on Samson, the cords falling off as burnt flax. The witnesses note that Gill notes Lehi, so called from what happened there, six miles from Etam. The witnesses note that the cords became as flax burnt with fire, and his bands loosed. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the Philistines shouted — against him; the the Spirit came mightily — the power; the the cords as burnt flax — the bands loosed; the the deliverer not held — not possible he should be holden (Acts 2:24).

The witnesses agree the bound deliverer could not be held when the Spirit came — "the Spirit of the LORD came mightily upon him, and the cords... became as flax that was burnt... and his bands loosed": at the shout of the foe, the power of God broke every cord as if it were burnt thread; the loosing of the bound shows that no chains can hold the man God empowers; it teaches that the LORD breaks the bonds of his servants and cannot have his deliverer holden, a faint figure of the greater Deliverer whom death itself could not bind, "it was not possible that he should be holden of it" (Acts 2:24), the cords falling from Samson as burnt flax.

So the witnesses make the verse the cords loosed: The witnesses note the the Philistines shouted: against him. The witnesses note the the Spirit came mightily: the power. The witnesses note the the cords as burnt flax: the bands loosed. The witnesses note the the deliverer not held: not possible he should be holden (Acts 2:24). The expositors observe the the Philistines shouted: against him. The expositors observe the the Spirit came mightily: the power. The expositors observe the the cords as burnt flax: the bands loosed. The expositors observe the the deliverer not held: not possible he should be holden (Acts 2:24). The believer is taught to mark the the Philistines shouted, the the Spirit came mightily, the the cords as burnt flax, the the deliverer not held, and to cry unto the LORD who gives water to the fainting. The verse thus records that the Spirit came mightily on Samson and the cords fell off as burnt flax — no chains holding the man God empowers, a figure of the greater Deliverer whom death could not bind, not possible that he should be holden of it (Acts 2:24).
15:
"And he found a new jawbone of an ass, and put forth his hand, and took it, and slew a thousand men therewith." The witnesses note Samson slaying a thousand Philistines with the jawbone of an ass. The witnesses note that Gill notes the jawbone of an ass lately killed, which perhaps had flesh upon it. The witnesses note that he slew a thousand men therewith. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the found a new jawbone — of an ass; the put forth his hand — took it; the slew a thousand men — therewith; the the weak confounds the mighty — god chose the weak to confound the mighty (1 Cor. 1:27).

The witnesses agree God wrought a mighty deliverance by the meanest of weapons — "he found a new jawbone of an ass... and slew a thousand men therewith": with no sword but a dead beast's jawbone, Samson, by the Spirit's strength, overthrew a thousand of the foe; the using of so base an instrument shows that God needs no great means to save, the victory plainly his when the weapon is so weak; it teaches that the LORD confounds the mighty by the weak and foolish things, that no flesh should glory, "God hath chosen the foolish things of the world to confound the wise... and the weak things... to confound the things which are mighty" (1 Cor. 1:27), Samson slaying a thousand with the jawbone.

So the witnesses make the verse the jawbone of an ass: The witnesses note the found a new jawbone: of an ass. The witnesses note the put forth his hand: took it. The witnesses note the slew a thousand men: therewith. The witnesses note the the weak confounds the mighty: God chose the weak to confound the mighty (1 Cor. 1:27). The expositors observe the found a new jawbone: of an ass. The expositors observe the put forth his hand: took it. The expositors observe the slew a thousand men: therewith. The expositors observe the the weak confounds the mighty: God chose the weak to confound the mighty (1 Cor. 1:27). The believer is taught to mark the found a new jawbone, the put forth his hand, the slew a thousand men, the the weak confounds the mighty, and to cry unto the LORD who gives water to the fainting. The verse thus records that Samson slew a thousand with the jawbone of an ass — God working mighty deliverance by the meanest weapon, confounding the mighty by the weak and foolish things, that no flesh should glory (1 Cor. 1:27).
16:
"And Samson said, With the jawbone of an ass, heaps upon heaps, with the jaw of an ass have I slain a thousand men." The witnesses note Samson's triumphant song over the slaughter with the jawbone. The witnesses note that Gill notes he said this in a kind of triumphant song. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the song, heaps upon heaps. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the with the jawbone — of an ass; the heaps upon heaps — the slain; the have I slain a thousand — his song; the beware self-glory — not unto us, but unto thy name give glory (Ps. 115:1).

The witnesses agree Samson burst into a song of triumph — "With the jawbone of an ass, heaps upon heaps... have I slain a thousand men": he celebrated the victory in a play on words, the slain lying in heaps; yet the repeated "have I slain" hints at a self-glorying that would soon be checked by his thirst; the song shows the joy of victory, but also the peril of ascribing too much to oneself; it teaches that the people of God should rejoice in his deliverances yet beware self-exaltation, giving the glory to him, "not unto us, O LORD, not unto us, but unto thy name give glory" (Ps. 115:1), Samson singing of the jawbone.

So the witnesses make the verse heaps upon heaps: The witnesses note the with the jawbone: of an ass. The witnesses note the heaps upon heaps: the slain. The witnesses note the have I slain a thousand: his song. The witnesses note the beware self-glory: not unto us, but unto thy name give glory (Ps. 115:1). The expositors observe the with the jawbone: of an ass. The expositors observe the heaps upon heaps: the slain. The expositors observe the have I slain a thousand: his song. The expositors observe the beware self-glory: not unto us, but unto thy name give glory (Ps. 115:1). The believer is taught to mark the with the jawbone, the heaps upon heaps, the have I slain a thousand, the beware self-glory, and to cry unto the LORD who gives water to the fainting. The verse thus records that Samson sang of the slaughter with the jawbone — the joy of victory, yet the repeated "I" hinting at self-glory soon checked, the people of God to give the glory to him, not unto us but unto thy name give glory (Ps. 115:1).
17:
"And it came to pass, when he had made an end of speaking, that he cast away the jawbone out of his hand, and called that place Ramathlehi." The witnesses note Samson casting away the jawbone and naming the place Ramath-lehi. The witnesses note that Gill notes the song probably consisted of more than is here expressed. The witnesses note that Ellicott notes Ramath-lehi, the hill of the jawbone. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the made an end of speaking — his song; the cast away the jawbone — the weapon; the Ramath-lehi — the hill of the jawbone; the a memorial kept — written for the generation to come (Ps. 102:18).

The witnesses agree Samson left a memorial of the victory in the place's name — "he cast away the jawbone... and called that place Ramathlehi": the "hill of the jawbone" would keep the memory of God's deliverance by so mean a weapon; the naming of the place shows the worth of memorials that recall the LORD's mercies; it teaches that the people of God do well to mark and name the scenes of his deliverances, that the memory be kept alive, "this shall be written for the generation to come" (Ps. 102:18), Samson naming Ramath-lehi.

So the witnesses make the verse Ramath-lehi: The witnesses note the made an end of speaking: his song. The witnesses note the cast away the jawbone: the weapon. The witnesses note the Ramath-lehi: the hill of the jawbone. The witnesses note the a memorial kept: written for the generation to come (Ps. 102:18). The expositors observe the made an end of speaking: his song. The expositors observe the cast away the jawbone: the weapon. The expositors observe the Ramath-lehi: the hill of the jawbone. The expositors observe the a memorial kept: written for the generation to come (Ps. 102:18). The believer is taught to mark the made an end of speaking, the cast away the jawbone, the Ramath-lehi, the a memorial kept, and to cry unto the LORD who gives water to the fainting. The verse thus records that Samson cast away the jawbone and named the place Ramath-lehi — a memorial of God's deliverance by a mean weapon, the people of God marking the scenes of his mercies, written for the generation to come (Ps. 102:18).
18:
"And he was sore athirst, and called on the LORD, and said, Thou hast given this great deliverance into the hand of thy servant: and now shall I die for thirst, and fall into the hand of the uncircumcised?" The witnesses note Samson, faint with thirst, crying to the LORD and owning the deliverance as his. The witnesses note that Gill notes Josephus thinks the thirst came as a rebuke for ascribing the victory to his own strength. The witnesses note that he owned, Thou hast given this great deliverance. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the he was sore athirst — brought low; the called on the LORD — in his need; the thou hast given — the victory owned his; the weakness felt — he giveth power to the faint (Isa. 40:29).

The witnesses agree the mighty victor was brought low by thirst, and turned to God — "he was sore athirst, and called on the LORD... Thou hast given this great deliverance": Gill notes Josephus held the thirst a rebuke for his self-glorying, that he might own his weakness; now he ascribes the victory rightly to God, "thou hast given," and cries to him in his need; the bringing-low of the strong man teaches that God humbles his servants to make them feel their dependence, and that the cry of weakness is heard, "he giveth power to the faint" (Isa. 40:29), Samson athirst and calling on the LORD.

So the witnesses make the verse sore athirst: The witnesses note the he was sore athirst: brought low. The witnesses note the called on the LORD: in his need. The witnesses note the thou hast given: the victory owned his. The witnesses note the weakness felt: he giveth power to the faint (Isa. 40:29). The expositors observe the he was sore athirst: brought low. The expositors observe the called on the LORD: in his need. The expositors observe the thou hast given: the victory owned his. The expositors observe the weakness felt: he giveth power to the faint (Isa. 40:29). The believer is taught to mark the he was sore athirst, the called on the LORD, the thou hast given, the weakness felt, and to cry unto the LORD who gives water to the fainting. The verse thus records that Samson, faint with thirst, cried to the LORD and owned the deliverance as his — God humbling his servant to feel his dependence after self-glory, the cry of weakness heard, he giveth power to the faint (Isa. 40:29).
19:
"But God clave an hollow place that was in the jaw, and there came water thereout; and when he had drunk, his spirit came again, and he revived: wherefore he called the name thereof Enhakkore..." The witnesses note God opening a spring, Samson reviving, and naming the place En-hakkore. The witnesses note that Gill notes God clave a hollow place, a socket in the form of a mortar, and water came out. The witnesses note that he called it En-hakkore, the well of him that cried. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the God clave a hollow place — the spring; the water came out — for the fainting; the his spirit revived — restored; the the well of him that cried — this poor man cried, and the LORD heard (Ps. 34:6).

The witnesses agree God answered the cry with a spring of water — "God clave an hollow place... there came water... his spirit came again, and he revived: wherefore he called the name thereof Enhakkore": the LORD opened water for the fainting man and restored his strength; the name "En-hakkore," "the well of him that cried," sealed the memory that God hears the cry of the needy; the giving of water in extremity teaches that God provides for his servants in their utmost need and answers the prayer of the faint, the well a witness that "this poor man cried, and the LORD heard him, and saved him out of all his troubles" (Ps. 34:6), the spring En-hakkore.

So the witnesses make the verse En-hakkore: The witnesses note the God clave a hollow place: the spring. The witnesses note the water came out: for the fainting. The witnesses note the his spirit revived: restored. The witnesses note the the well of him that cried: this poor man cried, and the LORD heard (Ps. 34:6). The expositors observe the God clave a hollow place: the spring. The expositors observe the water came out: for the fainting. The expositors observe the his spirit revived: restored. The expositors observe the the well of him that cried: this poor man cried, and the LORD heard (Ps. 34:6). The believer is taught to mark the God clave a hollow place, the water came out, the his spirit revived, the the well of him that cried, and to cry unto the LORD who gives water to the fainting. The verse thus records that God opened a spring and Samson revived, naming it En-hakkore, "the well of him that cried" — God providing for his servants in utmost need and answering the cry of the faint, this poor man cried and the LORD heard him (Ps. 34:6).
20:
"And he judged Israel in the days of the Philistines twenty years." The witnesses note Samson judging Israel twenty years in the days of the Philistines. The witnesses note that Gill notes he only began to deliver Israel, not completely, while the Philistines still had power. The witnesses note that he judged Israel twenty years. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the he judged Israel — twenty years; the in the days of the Philistines — still in power; the only began to deliver — partial; the a fuller Deliverer to come — he shall begin to deliver Israel (Judg. 13:5).

The witnesses agree the chapter closes with the measure of Samson's rule — "he judged Israel in the days of the Philistines twenty years": Gill notes he "only began to deliver them," not wholly freeing them while the Philistines yet held power; the limited and partial deliverance through Samson shows that the full salvation of Israel awaited another day; it teaches that the deliverers God raised up were but partial and temporary, pointing on to the one full and final Deliverer who would perfectly save his people, the angel having foretold that Samson should "begin to deliver Israel" (Judg. 13:5); thus the chapter ends, Samson judging Israel twenty years.

So the witnesses make the verse judged twenty years: The witnesses note the he judged Israel: twenty years. The witnesses note the in the days of the Philistines: still in power. The witnesses note the only began to deliver: partial. The witnesses note the a fuller Deliverer to come: he shall begin to deliver Israel (Judg. 13:5). The expositors observe the he judged Israel: twenty years. The expositors observe the in the days of the Philistines: still in power. The expositors observe the only began to deliver: partial. The expositors observe the a fuller Deliverer to come: he shall begin to deliver Israel (Judg. 13:5). The believer is taught to mark the he judged Israel, the in the days of the Philistines, the only began to deliver, the a fuller Deliverer to come, and to cry unto the LORD who gives water to the fainting. The verse thus records that Samson judged Israel twenty years, beginning but not completing their deliverance — the partial deliverers pointing on to the one full and final Deliverer, Samson to begin to deliver Israel out of the hand of the Philistines (Judg. 13:5).
